1.label: Question 1
1.scenario: A change in health insurance regulation will, in the future, make it mandatory for private health insurers to introduce a portability clause in their policy contract. The portability clause assures that in case the policyholder is changing his insurer, the accrued rights (like coverage of pre-existing conditions) under the old contract are not lost and transferred to the new policy contract. However, the insurers are allowed to assess each risk as per their underwriting policy.

i.question: Give reasons why policyholder may want to change the insurer.

Indicative Solution: Premium increase
Bad service (slow policy issuance, late claim payment, poor call center experience, bad reputation)
Better fitting product offered by the competition

ii.question: Please describe accrued rights a policyholder can lose when changing the insurer in the Indian context. What are the practical challenges for an insurer when accepting the accrued rights from the older contract? You may give some examples.

Indicative Solution: Claim free bonus e.g. increased sum insured
Coverage of pre-existing conditions
Coverage of specific illnesses
Coverage of pregnancy

Practical challenges: Non-standardized definitions and non-standardized benefits in the market. Different rules for sum insured bonus in case of claim free years. Different definitions for pre-existing conditions or different exclusion clause related to pre-existing conditions.

iii.question: Please describe the potential impact of such a clause to the: (i) Policyholders of rather good health (ii) Policyholders who are chronically sick

Indicative Solution: Good health: The insured can assume that he will be accepted as a standard risk by any other health insurer since he is healthy. Hence, change of the insurer will be easier since accrued rights will not get lost, e.g. coverage of pre-existing conditions, or claim free bonus will be transferred to the new contract. Hence the healthy insured can change to the insurer of his choice because the new insurer is cheaper or provides the better service.

Chronically sick: The insured must assume that he will not be accepted as standard risk - due to his health condition and hence he may get confronted with a discouraging offer from competitors. This makes it likely that the insured will stay in the existing portfolio. In case insurer is losing more healthy risks - which is likely when he increases premiums or because of lacking service - the premium for the remaining portfolio, e.g. the chronically sick insured - may need to even further increase.

iv.question: Please describe the risks and chances to the health insurers related to the regulatory change described above.

Indicative Solution: Risks: Increased anti-selective lapse especially when premiums are required to be adjusted. Obligation to write more fresh business to keep the risk profile of the portfolio at the same level. Additional complexity in underwriting to cope with the accrued rights of changing policyholders. Substantially increased administration expenses since product specific rules like waiting periods, claim free bonuses are not followed. Risk of not being able to recover initial expenses. Increased risk of fraud and error. Increased pricing risk since portfolio is more exposed to competitor's behavior.
Chances: Strong incentive to provide good services and keep the premiums low. Improved reputation. Companies successful can capture more quickly market share and beat the competition.

v.question: How will your answer under d) change in case the insurance companies would not be allowed to underwrite insured's – who were covered under a policy with similar benefits before?

Indicative Solution: The insurance companies own underwriting policy could be violated by the regulatory change. Companies with efficient underwriting approach and good service standards could now attract sub-standard risks without getting the adequate premium. The chances could be shifted to companies with less favorable claims service standards.

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario: An insurer plans to introduce a new inpatient health insurance product for individuals in the Indian market. The product development team has now to decide if the product should have the cashless facility or if it should be rather a reimbursement product only.

i.question: Please discuss the pros and cons of the cashless facility feature.

Indicative Solution: Pros: Client friendly feature and hence product easier to sell. Cashless feature requires contractual relationship to providers, e.g. a provider network. The claims pre-authorization process bears basically the chance to control claims better. Pre-agreed fee schedules or package rates allow the insurer to manage the ongoing medical inflation better. A quality provider network provides the opportunity to use their brand to help sell the product.

Cons: Cashless feature may increase the utilization by insured's, hence higher claims costs. Administrative burden to manage network. Cost of maintaining network. Risk of fraud through collusion with hospitals.

ii.question: What would be your advice? Please explain the rationale behind your advice.

Indicative Solution: Recommend having cashless facility due to customer convenience, better claims control through pre-authorization, negotiated tariffs, competitive advantage. However, should have proper checks and balances to avoid misuse.

i.question: Please list the main risk of a health insurer in general.

Indicative Solution: Insurance risk (morbidity and mortality). Market risk. Credit risk. Operational risk. Liquidity risk. Regulatory risk. Catastrophe risk. Pricing risk. Reserving risk. Reinsurance risk.

ii.question: The risks listed under a) are generally applicable to non-health insurers too. Please explain five most specific health risks in the current Indian market context and give examples for each of those risks.

Indicative Solution: Anti-selection: Due to information asymmetry, unhealthy people more likely to buy insurance.
Moral hazard: Insured may overuse medical services or inflate claims.
Provider induced demand: Doctors/hospitals may recommend unnecessary tests/treatments.
Pre-existing conditions: Long waiting periods lead to disputes. Some conditions are never covered.
Medical inflation: Rapid increase in healthcare costs outpacing premium increases.
Fraud: Fake claims, double claiming, collusion with providers.
Concentration risk: Too much exposure to certain regions or demographic segments.

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario: Give the formulae for the following measures of a PMI product portfolio based on occurrence year basis and give relationship between them: Incidence Rate, Average Claim Size, Burning Cost per Capita, Premium per Life, Loss Ratio

i.question: Give the formulae for the measures and give relationship between them.

Indicative Solution: Incidence Rate = Number of claims / Number of lives exposed
Average Claim Size = Total claims amount / Number of claims
Burning Cost per Capita = Incidence Rate x Average Claim Size
Premium per Life = Gross Premium / Number of lives
Loss Ratio = Total Claims / Total Premium
Burning Cost per Capita / Premium per Life = Loss Ratio

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario: Explain the meaning of the following terms related to Health Insurance:

i.question: Explain: a) Usual, Customary & Reasonable Charges b) Preferred Provider Network c) Age-at-entry pricing d) Community Rating

Indicative Solution: UCR: Charges that are consistent with prevailing rates in a geographic area for similar services.
PPN: Network of healthcare providers who have agreed to provide services at negotiated rates.
Age-at-entry pricing: Pricing based on the age when the insured first enters the policy, with no age-related increases thereafter.
Community Rating: Same premium charged to everyone in a community regardless of age, health status, or other risk factors.

6.label: Question 6
6.scenario: The following data relates to the one year renewable health insurance policies with typical inpatient indemnity benefit for individual retail line of business of a mid-sized insurance company which monitors the performance of its products on Policy Year basis: 2005: 41% LR, 124094 policies; 2006: 55% LR, 72843; 2007: 67% LR, 54188; 2008: 78% LR, 45692; 2009: 87% LR, 40876; 2010: 92% LR, 39021

i.question: What do you understand by Policy Year Loss Ratio?

Indicative Solution: Policy Year Loss Ratio is the ratio of incurred claims to earned premium for policies written in a particular year, tracked over subsequent policy years as they renew.

ii.question: What could be the reasons of the upward trend in performance as given in the table?

Indicative Solution: Anti-selection - healthier policies lapsing. Inadequate pricing. Medical inflation. Changes in coverage/benefits. Poor claims management. Increased competition. Regulatory changes.

iii.question: What are the limitations of monitoring experience by Policy Year basis?

Indicative Solution: Doesn't reflect current business. Long time to develop. May not capture product changes. Mix may change over time.

7.label: Question 7
7.scenario: What are the customer needs met by Long Term Care Insurance (LTCI) products? Considering the trends in demographic, political and social factors in last 2-3 decades discuss in detail the potential of LTCI products in India.

i.question: Discuss the customer needs and potential of LTCI products in India.

Indicative Solution: Customer needs: Medical and custodial care for elderly. Financial protection against long-term care costs. Independence and dignity in old age. Relief for family caregivers.

Potential: Aging population - increased life expectancy. Nuclear families - less family support. Rising healthcare costs. Limited government provision. Growing middle class with awareness. Tax benefits. Low penetration currently - high growth potential.

8.label: Question 8
8.scenario: You are heading the actuarial department of a medium sized insurance company in India which deals in various health insurance products. The following data relates to the one year renewable inpatient indemnity product for individual retail line of business. No price revision has taken place in the last 3 years. Data: Occurrence Year 2008-09: GWP 741, EP 435, Reported Claims 298; 2009-10: GWP 1626, EP 1438, Reported Claims 904; 2010-11: GWP 2523, EP 1954, Reported Claims 1293. Loss Development Factors (cumulative) for 3 years as 1.178, 1.021 and 1.000. Annual trend of 2% in utilization and 8% in inflation. Loadings: Business Acquisition 10%, Claims Handling 5%, Management 10%, Profit & Contingency 5%.

i.question: You are required to recommend the overall adjustment in premium based on the information given above.

Indicative Solution: Calculate ultimate claims for each year using development factors. Calculate earned premium. Calculate actual loss ratio for each year. Apply trend factors to project future claims. Calculate overall projected loss ratio. Compare with target loss ratio to determine required rate change.

ii.question: When do you think such kind of approach is appropriate for re-pricing? Which other approach is generally followed?

Indicative Solution: Appropriate when: Adequate data available, stable portfolio, experience is credible. Other approach: Pure premium method, loss ratio method, composite rating.

iii.question: Do you think that the portfolio will exactly behave in the way you have predicted in your calculation after implementing the rate change? What are the factors if any that may have impact on portfolio not behaving as expected?

Indicative Solution: No, portfolio may not behave exactly as predicted. Factors: Competition, economic conditions, regulatory changes, product changes, anti-selection, catastrophe, claim inflation, data quality.

iv.question: The Marketing Director feels that the rate revision recommended may affect the prospects adversely. After discussions it was decided to increase overall rates by 15% across the board. What will be the resulting loss ratio for the coverage period with 15% rate change?

Indicative Solution: If rates increase by 15%, the loss ratio will decrease by approximately 13% (1/1.15). Actual calculation depends on base loss ratio.
